content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I need more really good movies from the 40s-60s

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I want to see more older (black and white or color) movies from the 40s-60s.  
    

What I Have Seen And Loved:  
Rear Window  
Ace In The Hole  
Double Indemnity  
Sunset Boulevard  
Treasure of The Seria Madre  
39 Steps  
Strangers On A Train  
Veritigo  
Modern Times  
Gold Rush  
Gilda  
The Red Shoes  
It Happend One Night  
All That Heaven Allows

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Best Years of Our Lives

Casablanca

The Maltese Falcon

Some Like It Hot

Singin' in the Rain

City Lights (from the 1930s, but fits with the other Chaplin films)
2. North by Northwest (1959)
3. Orson Welles is a huge omission here! You have to watch Touch of Evil and The Third Man
4. Maltese Falcon

Casablanca 

All About Eve

Some Like it Hot

Lawrence of Arabia

Doctor Zhivago

Bridge on the River Kwai

Edited to add the David Lean joints
5. Pscho 1960

The Heiress 1949

My Cousin Rachel 1952

Laura 1944

Charade 1963

Imitation of Life 1959

Gaslight 1944

12 Angry Men 1957


6. The Bad Seed

East of Eden

All About Eve

Rififi

Casablanca

Diabolique

Edit- typo
7. *The Wages of Fear* and *The Night of the Hunter* are both on my list. Just watched *Persona* a couple months ago for the first time, that was nice. And if you stretch a little further into the 60s, *Repulsion* blew me away as a teen and I still love it to this day.
8. The Apartment

Desk Set

The More the Merrier

Notorious (1946)
9. Seven Samurai (1954) or any Akira Kurosawa film, for that matter.
10. The African Queen

Fantasia

## 2. Movies that take place over a short time span?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Hey, I am looking for movies that take place over a short time period like a day or a week. A movie that comes to mind right now is Kate (2021). I am looking for a particular genre. So drop any recommendations you have! Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Run Lola Run (1998). Takes place over 20 minutes (not real time though). 
2. Before Sunset. The movie is a little over an hour long and takes place mostly in real time. If would require having watched the previous movie, Before Sunrise. 
3. 12 angry men (1957) - a single evening.
4. Locke (2013) takes place during one night and the whole film is just a man driving and having phone calls with different people lol. 

But I thought it was great, awesome performance from Tom Hardy. 
5. Ferris Beuller's Day Off
6. Groundhog Day, same day, every day.
7. Dazed & Confused takes place on the last day of school before summer. 
8. La haine and recently watched rye lane too 
9. Birdman
10. The man from earth, The coffee table

for both I highly recommand to see them without spoilers

## 3. Movies where an old person saves the day using obsolete technology

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I had a conversation recently about COBOL, a "dead" programming language that is still very much around and important, but not being taught to young programmers, which made got me thinking.       
Are there any movies about an old(er) person or people who have to save the day or solve some problem because they are the only ones left who remember some old legacy system or outdated method for doing something? I was able to find *Space Cowboys* (2000) but couldn't find anything else that really fit.

I'm not looking for "old thief still has what it takes to pull a heist" movies like *Entrapment* or "old warrior is still a badass" movies like *RED* unless it is a specific obsolete skill that is needed and they're the only one who still uses it. Not movies that are generally "old people can still be cool".

Thanks.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. IndependenceDay !!
2. Battleship. While not the main characters, there's a cool sequence where WW2 vets aboard the USS Missouri recommission it out of a museum to fight aliens. Reasons are explained in the movie. Don't want to say too much and spoil. 
  - I'll add that I believe the vets in the movie were actual WW2 Navy vets
  - The montage is my favorite part of that silly movie 
3. The Rock definitely fits your criteria.

They have to go drag Sean Connery’s character out of prison to break into Alcatraz specifically because he was the only one ever to escape and they needed his skills.
4. Star Trek IV: Hunt For Nuclear Wessels
  - \-Computer. Computer? Hello computer.

\-Just use the keyboard.

\-Keyboard. How quaint.
5. Mars Attacks!?
6. Not a movie but series, Battlestar Galactica.
7. The Lost Boys & Granpa. 

## 4. Movies that are disturbing, not necessarily because it's gross or gory, but because it makes you confront an ugly part of humanity

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Obsession is huge right now, in great part because it forces a conversation about masculinity and gender relations. 

&#x200B;

But beyond the discourse, the movie changed the way I view the world; on how many times I've acted, or seen people act like the characters.

&#x200B;

I'm looking for movies that say the monster is already inside everyone, but in a way that can actually change my outlook on life, not just "the monster is a metaphor for mental illness".

&#x200B;

Besides Obsession, I got this paradigm shift after watching Possession, Lake Mungo, City of God, Perfect Blue, Speak no Evil (original). All of these movies made me face the ugliness around and within me, and I'm searching for more. Thanks for reading this far down, and sorry if I'm a little long winded.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Zone of Interest. Haunting film.
2. [**COMPLIANCE**](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/84188-compliance#play=cDat96UyT5A)

It's a true story. Look it up after you watch the movie.
  - I never saw the movie but I saw the documentary as well, I majored in psychology and this film is disturbing as to the things people can be manipulated into. 
3. Does Don't Look Up count?
  - I would agree
4. Red Rooms
5. *I'm Thinking of Ending Things* 

*Synecdoche, New York*

Both by Charlie Kaufman and both come across like a nightmare / fever dream that you're very uncomfortable from afterwards. 


6. Sling Blade

Dogviile

Midnight Express 

Return to Paradise 

Deliverance 

Schindler's List 


7. Aniara (2017)  
The Divide (2011)  
Blindness (2008)  
The Road (2009)
8. Threads

The Painted Bird

Come and See. 

## 5. Looking for war movies that show the main character/s phyche deteriorating throughout the story

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I finished playing through Spec Ops: The Line, a video game in which the main character is sent on a millitary mission. Long story short, things go off the rails fast, the main character does more harm than good and tries to convince himself that it's all worth it. His mind is in such rough state that hallucinations become common through the story and blend with his reality.

Which movies do you suggest like that, which show a more traumatic personal journey?

Don’t worry, i know i’m probably very uncultured in movies. I'm already aware that there's probably TONS of movies like that, but i've never watched too many war movies (Downfall, Platoon, Full metal jacket, letters from iwo jima, hacksaw ridge and My Way, are the ones that come to mind that i've seen).

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Apocalypse Now

The Deer Hunter
  - Another vote for The Deer Hunter. Walken and De Niro give some great performances in this one.
  - The one and only answer for a great movie that shows the mental toll war and killing have a the human psyche. 

Other honorable mentions:  
Full Metal Jacket  
Deer Hunter  
Platoon  
Born on the 4th of July

\*Outside of Apocalypse Now and Full Metal Jacket, I’m remembering from memory, not because I saw them recently. 

Hope this helps.
2. Come and See. His psyche definitely umm deteriorates 
3. Platoon 
4. All quiet on the western front 
5. Deer hunter
6. Check out Jacob's Ladder. Not quite what your request was, but it was among the inspo for Spec Ops: The line. 

Generation Kill, a tv show, also does a pretry good job at showing soldiers who kind of grapple with thinking their doing the right thing/wrong thing, or if its just following orders, etc. 
  - *Generation Kill* is an awesome show.  It’s a great look at the bureaucracy of warfare and the absurdity of the chain of command.  Really compelling stuff.   
7. Jarhead 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What's your favorite movie dialogue that explores humanity or the nature of people? My favorite is this dialogue between Agent K and Agent J in Men in Black.

**Score:** 3414

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's no dialogue.  My favorite MIB scene is Agent J trying out and having to take a written test without a hard surface to write on.  Dragging the only table from the center of the room over to his chair is great visual storytelling of his practical street smarts and shamelessness, while also being funny too.  It also contrasts well with the military mindset and conformity of the others.
  - Mine's the shooting scene. "May I ask why you felt little Tiffany deserved to die?"
    - https://preview.redd.it/pvmuv4153s8h1.jpeg?width=398&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6667cf772dfb5598354aef836e5450265de1eda2
    - "And I realized, he's not snarling...he's *sneezing*."
    - Those books are way too advanced for her!
    - "So I'd appreciate if you'd ease up off my back 'bout it. [*Beat*] Or do I owe her an apology?"
  - My husband and I say, "You wanna get down on this?" if we're offering to share something.
  - I love how they all look at him and pass judgment but refuse to use the table.
2. I wish it need not have happened in my time," said Frodo.
"So do I," said Gandalf, "and so do all who live to see such times. But that is not for them to decide. All we have to decide is what to do with the time that is given us.
  - The dialogue before this is very good as well. I’m sure I’m butchering it but it goes something like 

Frodo- “It’s a pity bilbo didn’t kill gollum”

Gandalf- “pity? It was pity that stayed Bilbos hand. Many who have died deserve life, and some who live may deserve death. Can you give it to them Frodo?”

## 2. Obsession (2026) is one of the most disturbing films I’ve seen in years

**Score:** 1590
**Body:** For the sake of spoilers, I can’t say much about this movie other than it fucked me up (in a good way).

Well-crafted horror movies like this will eventually put the horror genre back on track and garner Oscar recognition not only for technical awards, but for writing, acting, and directing too.

Hard to believe that 26-year-old film auteur Curry Barker made it for $750,000 against a $300 million (to date) box office take.

Makes me look forward to seeing more fresh work from Barker in the future. Horror’s coming back!

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I've been looking for a good "date night" movie
  - This is the one. It’s well worth the price of admission.
    - $6.99
2. Not to split hairs, but "put the horror genre back on track?" Horror flicks have been killing it for years. Obsession seems like another very welcome symptom of a greater current that I want to keep happily consuming.
  - Hereditary, talk to me, bring her back, the wailing, smile, the mummy, so many good horror movies the past few years. These are only the ones I thought of off the top of my head
    - The mummy was awful I don’t understand how Reddit seems to like it. 
  - Yeah horror has been excellent since 2014, and it fully kicked into gear with Hereditary and Get Out, with each year giving more and more horror classics. In the last few years we’ve had Sinners, Weapons, Bring Her Back, The Substance, Nosferatu, Longlegs, Talk To Me, M3GAN, Barbarian, Pearl, X and countless others that have been big commercial successes and received acclaim. This year alone in addition to Obsession we’ve had Send Help, Bone Temple, Undertone, Hokum, Backrooms and Leviticus, and while not every movie will hit the same or resonate, there’s essentially something for every horror fan to enjoy, and we’ve still got Evil Dead Burn, Resident Evil and Werewulf on the horizon along with another potential breakout that we’re not even really aware of yet
3. Horror has been back for at least a decade now. At least Obsession's kind of horror. This, maybe as of yet unofficial sub-genre, which exists in a state between arthouse and mainstream, which is this that you defined as "accessibility", and its each entry keeps a nice and tidy budget while being highly original and independent. I can pinpoint the first noticeable movies of this new genre in 2014 with the Babadook and It Follows and then becoming firmly established with the arrival of A24 the next year and Robert Eggers' the Witch. There were plenty of movies like that since then such as Hereditary, Midsommar, the Lighthouse, Barbarian, Weapons, Talk to me, Bring her back, Get out, Us, Hokum, the Substance, Longlegs,   Oddity and now Obsession too, among many others.
4. I think it's kind of funny
5. I haven't seen Obsession, I am very much looking forward to it.. but to read so many of the same "it's the most disturbing movie I've ever seen" posts, I just have a hard time believing it's THAT good/crazy.  I've seen just about every new, old, obscure horror movie there is.. so seems like a bit of an exaggeration ngl.

## 3. What's a movie that got a sequel that was unnecessary and far inferior to the first? I have two picks: Horrible Bosses and Neighbors (2014). Awful sequels, truly garbage.

**Score:** 308

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Independence Day 2
2. Gladiator 2
3. There is no better answer than…Highlander 2.  
  - There can be only one 
4. I agree with Neighbors, but Horrible Bosses 2 was pretty fucking funny. On the same level as the first one 1000%

It's not like the first one was revolutionary in any way, the ending is so so cringe and dumb and is full of plot holes.

Horrible bosses 2 was the better "plot" movie, but the first Horrible Bosses movie is funnier. 

If Horrible Bosses gets a B+ then the sequel deserves nothing less than a B- 


5. Man I’m sure glad Pacific Rim doesn’t fall under this category 😮‍💨
  - yeah good thing they only ever made just one movie!
6. Dumb and Dumberer or Dumb and Dumber To, whichever you consider the true sequel.
7. Zoolander
8. https://preview.redd.it/4d5k5vr14v8h1.jpeg?width=1728&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=97bb507f7033b4fe5add5d58854b89f50f518fbd

## 4. What's an ending that ruined an otherwise fine movie for you? For me, it's definitely Crazy, Stupid, Love. It is such BS that Steve's character had to apologize at the end. The wife was the one at fault. She is not worth anyone's sympathy.

**Score:** 275

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's called Crazy Stupid Love, not Reasonable Fair Love.
  - pretty friggin fair tbh
2. The theatrical ending of I am legend was legendary bad. The alternative ending was so much better, when he realized he wasn't the hero he thought he was
  - I remember seeing that in the theater and thinking during the movie how good it was, and the interesting take that they were setting up, that he was a monster. And then, they just ignored all of that set up for some weird ass ending, even going so far is to not include a scene that was in the trailers. Like what the fuck?

This will always be the poster child for fucked up endings to me. I don't know whose idea that was, but they really screwed the pooch on that one.
3. 100% agreed OP. 

That movie was such a fun watch then they spin it to make the guy who got cheated on the bad guy. 
4. Law Abiding Citizen is the ultimate answer
5. Pay It Forward. He didn't have to die.
  - I felt for the character for getting stabbed, but I was moreso angry that the movie just completely glossed over that the other kid fucking *killed him*. No scene at all about the kid getting arrested, not even a mention. The bully just became a fucking murderer and the movie just goes "Let's cry over the victim and focus on the acts of kindness and *completely ignore* that  what the other kid did was so reprehensible."  

I get what the moral focus was (hell, it's in the title) but for the life of me I can't get over the idea that kid-on-kid murder is so glibly tossed aside to focus on the protagonist's legacy of kind acts. Decades later and the thought of that ending still pisses me off .
6. I thought you were talking about the babysitter giving the kid the photos.. that ruined it for me.. That little creeper gets rewarded at the end and the babysitter now comes off as ... weirder/gross? 
7. Longlegs. 

## 5. So I watched The Running Man (Glen Powell)

**Score:** 193
**Body:** So I was scrolling on amazon prime yesterday and found this movie. It looked interesting enough so I gave it a try since I liked edgar wright's other movies. It's a pretty good action film! The acting was alright (except for a few line deliveries here or there) and the cinematography was pretty cool. Glenn Powell does a good job as Richard's but I couldn't take him seriously most of the time (like when he tried to be angry, his face just looked kinda dumb 🤣) the villians were okay at best but they weren't as threatening besides a couple of scenes) the movie itself is well shot and has pretty good writing, it's all around a decent action flick that 10 year old me would've loved. What are your thoughts about it?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I prefer the OG for the mere mindless spectacle, absudity, and Richard Dawson of it all. 

This one was a solid action vehicle that followed the book more faithfully but still botched the ending in comparison to the novel. I never need to see it again, but I def watch the OG version from time to time still. 
  - ![gif](giphy|12Rq9uCNxcxtjq)
  - OG also has Mick Fleetwood and Yaphet Kotto
    - And Jesse "The Body" Ventura 
      - And Jim Brown! 
  - I was so super-excited when I read that this movie would be much more book-faithful.

Watching the movie, for like 90% of the runtime it was largely faithful. Movies always have to trim some details books have, since movies have time constraints, so that was fine. Some other changes were made to let Wright inject humor, okay, np, that’s his whole jam.

But then at the end it deviates from the book pretty significantly.

It’s s good movie, but I’m 99% sure I’d have liked it a lot more if I hadn’t read the book, and went in expecting to largely experience the book on-screen.
  - The OG one is great because it was made before the great "greyification" of America.

I miss colors.....
2. Michael Cera was great in this
3. I prefer the original but I still enjoyed it. Apparently it’s much more accurate with the book but I never read it. 
4. Way better than I thought it was gonna be, but at no point did it feel like an Edgar Wright movie, and that was a bummer.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Stalker (1979) - My personal take

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I managed to reach the age of 52 without ever having seen this movie. I finally caught up with it on a quiet afternoon off, while resting after donating blood. A very well-spent afternoon, I must say.

After watching the film, here is my take on it (spoilers ahead):

The three protagonists clearly move as three archetypes: Science for the Professor, Art for the Writer, and Faith for the Stalker. Art and science move forward driven by logic, profit, or intellectual ambition, while faith acts as a guide because it is the only one equipped to explore the soul. Yet, in front of the Room of Desires, everything comes to a halt. The threshold is insurmountable not due to a physical danger, but out of sheer terror of the truth: the Room does not grant what a person claims to want, but rather the deepest, most subconscious essence of their being. Porcupine's suicide is the definitive proof of this. The Room stripped him of his moral alibis and forced him to face his own truth, revealing that beneath his superficial altruism lay a gut-level selfishness that only desired wealth.

However, the journey is by no means a failure. Contrary to what the Stalker believes, the Writer and the Professor undergo a genuine reality check and return from the Zone better than they entered. Choosing not to cross the threshold is their greatest act of maturity. The Writer drops his mask of destructive cynicism and accepts his own vulnerability; the Professor dismantles the bomb, relinquishing the scientific pretense of controlling or destroying what he cannot comprehend. They looked into the abyss, understood their limits, and chose silence and intellectual honesty over the arrogance of action.

The one who truly emerges defeated from the experience is the Stalker himself, and this is where the film confronts the most tragic paradox of faith. From the very beginning, when we see him coldly closing the bedroom door, leaving his wife and sick daughter in the dark, we catch a glimpse of his deep-seated selfishness. The Stalker is a man blinded by his own dogma: he desperately seeks the miracle and the sacred in an alternate, abstract, and idealized place, becoming completely numb to the reality surrounding him. He *needs* humanity to be desperate and hopeless to justify his own mission and spiritual ego. When his companions refuse to enter, he collapses not out of compassion for them, but because he finds himself stripped of his role as a savior.

Tarkovsky exposes this blindness through the family dynamics and the elements that invade the house in the final scenes:

* **The Wife's Evolution:** The contrast between her desperate screams at the beginning and her immense dignity at the end reveals that she is the only true spiritual center of the film. She has walked through the pain, accepted her husband's obsessive nature, and chosen to stand by him anyway. In her final monologue, speaking directly to the audience, she describes a "bitter happiness" and embodies the only force that doesn't need the Zone to exist: unconditional love that shoulders the suffering of others. She doesn't need miracles because she creates the meaning of life every single day.
* **The Telekinesis Scene:** The ending with little Monkey moving glasses with her mind, which might look like a cheap sci-fi gimmick at first glance, is actually a brilliant piece of visual irony. It is the director's way of screaming at the Stalker (and at us) about his guilty distraction: he spent his entire life breaching borders and risking his life to find a miracle in the Zone, completely failing to realize that the inexplicable, the sacred, and the extraordinary were always right under his nose, sitting at his own dinner table.
* **The Presence of the Black Dog:** The dog that appears out of nowhere in the Zone and later ends up curled at the foot of the bed, without anyone being surprised by its presence, serves as the ultimate bridge. It proves that the separation between the gray world of everyday life and the magical Zone is merely an illusion of the Stalker's mind. Mystery is a constant presence pervading reality; the Stalker is simply too busy idolizing a sacred enclosure to notice that the absolute is already walking by his side.

In short, the film shows how obsessive faith can blind a person, driving them to chase an abstract idea of salvation while preventing them from seeing the tangible miracles of everyday reality.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It doesn't make a lot of sense as an interpretation to me:

1) It was not Tarkovsky's own interpretation in "Sculpting in Time".

2) If we see the Porcupine story as true as you seemingly do, does this not contradict the notion that the Stalker's ideas about the zone are mere abstract dogma?

3) The telekinesis scene does not come from nowhere; it is heavily implied to be specifically an effect of the zone on the children of Stalkers - that is to say, there is in fact something truly miraculous about the zone.

4) I see no evolution in the wife, only a cyclical struggle and acceptance which could arise time and again when the Stalker leaves.

5) Somewhat similarly, cynicism triumphs in the writer. I don't see how you think he has let it go. His cynicism makes him lose all hope of betterment because it pervades even his perception of the zone and of the Stalker. Having no faith in anything, he is left without hope as well.

6) The material environment of the characters in the mundane world seems pretty degrading and desperate to me without reference to any "need" of the Stalker to be the saviour of humanity.
  - > It was not Tarkovsky's own interpretation

Could we not? Art is a conversation between the creator and the perceiver... both sides have inherent value.
2. I mostly agree with your take on the core of the characters' psychology, especially regarding their refusal to enter the Room. It’s a great reading of the film.

We align with the grounded, non-allegorical interpretation of the myth of the zone; the act of turning back, and somewhat on the telekinesis ending:

You noted that the threshold isn't insurmountable due to physical danger. I take this a step further, in that despite all of the Stalker's terrifying warnings and his ritual of throwing metal nuts, *none* of the invisible traps ever actually materialize. This introduces a major ambiguity: is the Zone actually supernatural, or is it a collective delusion that the Stalker psychologically depends on to give himself a sense of purpose?

Your point about the Writer and Professor returning "better" because they chose silence over action fits perfectly with the film's structure. During that famous five-minute long take outside the Room, their refusal to play along completely dismantles the Stalker’s spiritual ego**.**

While you view the daughter's telekinesis as a brilliant piece of visual irony, I actually think of it as a bit of a dud or a "cheap parlor trick." I argue that if minor telekinesis is the absolute best the Zone can offer humanity, it ultimately validates the deep skepticism held by the Writer and the Professor all along.

Either way, looking at the film through a pragmatic, psychological lens rather than a purely spiritual one makes the character dynamics much more compelling.
3. When you read Roadside Picnic by Arkady and Boris Strugatsky a lot of the purposefully undefined and abstract aspects of the film are given some context inasmuch as the book was a framework for the screenplay (which was also adapted by the book's authors). 

I've read it three times now. Its one of my favorites. 

I've seen the film countless times, and I'm still enchanted and mesmerized by it from start to finish. 
4. Great write-up. I came to Stalker late too and it hits different when youre not coming from the art-house side of things. What got me is how Tarkovsky builds more tension in a single tracking shot than most action movies do in their whole runtime. The Zone doesnt need jump scares or gunfights — the atmosphere does all the work. The telekinesis reveal at the end floored me.
5. I'm not sure how much I agree with this analysis, but points for originality!

For what its worth, my interpretation was that the Stalker himself had once entered the Room, and the innermost desire it granted him was his child.
6. I really like this take. As with all great pieces of art, there can be multiple interpretations, it is not a 2d pixel art, the characters are complex, the visual impact is variable, little details can be noted or ignored. 

Stalker as Faith actually makes so much sense, I never thought of him (or them) as representing Faith. It actually explains his collapse - he brings people to the Zone not because that is how he makes a living (evidently, doesn't exactly live in luxury despite being the guide to a fairy tale genie), but because he believes in taking people to the sacred place. Maybe not a Saviour, but certainly some variation of a Prophet or other faith-based persona. Actually, one sacred being in Buddhism ticks the box: Bodhisattva (Buddhism): Compassionate, enlightened beings who postpone their own final entry into nirvana to save and guide all suffering beings to enlightenment (wikipedia definition). He postpones his own entry to guide others. Obviously, it is highly unlikely Tarkovksy meant Bodhissatva or even knew about them. 

I also like the interpretation of the Monkey's final scene - it has puzzled me for a long time, since the first time I watched decades ago (and was super disappointed in the film, being a teenager and expecting a sci-fi movie). Irrespective of the fact that it is likely the impact of the Stalker's life (although could be the impact of proximity to the Zone), at least one interpretation that he regularly escapes to find the miracle while he is so close to the miracle in his own family. 

On a different note, the Porcupine's story is absolutely heart-breaking. It is such a powerful and short tale which twists the millenia-old wish-granting tale into a completely new tragic meaning. It is actually taken without changes from the source book by Strugatski brothers as it is so central to everything (a lot of things havw been changed from the book to the film, despite Strugatski brothers also writing the script). It has been stuck in my head for such a long time, it is an extremely powerful and terrifying tale. 

Interpretation does not need to match exactly to what the director had in mind when creating a piece of art. The more complex the art is the more diverse are its interpretations. Art goes to on to live the life of its own after being created.

## 2. Anthology film segments that become more famous than the movies they belong to.

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I've seen this happen a couple of times, where some segments become famous on their own, with a lot of people assuming that they are self-contained shorts without being aware of the movie they originally come from.  I think Fellini's Toby Dammit, Pasolini's La ricotta and Godard's Dans le noir du temps fall within this category. Does anyone know any other examples?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I once saw someone here on Reddit describe Tarkovsky's Andrei Rublev as "a documentary about how to cast a church bell in medieval Russia". I do think it's the most famous sequence by far, although maybe not quite famous on its own.
2. Sorcerer's Apprentice from Disney's Fantasia. To this day it's probably one of the most iconic depictions of Mickey Mouse. And the sorcerer Mickey hat has become a recognizable piece of Disney iconography, appearing in the Disney parks. It stands well on its own because it's pretty much just an early Mickey Mouse cartoon episode. 
3. The Mickey Mouse wizard segment from Fantasia is a lot more famous than the rest of the movie. I think it’s been available as a standalone short on TV and DVDs over the years. I at least have strong memories of watching it growing up, though I don’t think I ever saw the full movie until a couple years back where I didn’t really recognise any of the other segments. So many beautiful scenes in it though, but it’s hard to deny that it’s the most iconic. 
  - Yeah that's definitely the most famous. But Night on Bald Mountain is a close second. I have seen so many stills and clips from it online.
4. truffaut's antoine & colette - originally from the 1962 intl anthology film l'amour à vingt ans (AKA love at twenty)

which isn't really surprising as it's the longest and best segment by a long shot, and as far as i know the full anthology film is only available as an italian dubbed DVD (ie. almost impossible to watch the other segments in HD)
5. I think "Magnetic Rose" is a good example of that. It's the first of 3 films in the animated anthology "Memories", produced by Katsuhiro Otomo, of *Akira* fame. While all 3 of the short stories is worth watching, i believe the anthology has become a cult classic mostly because of Magnetic Rose, which is by far the most interesting piece. It also has Satoshi Kon as screenwriter, which has a huge cult following. For many, having both Otomo and Kon collaborating together is basically a dream come true, so that one particular short film overshadows the other two.

A more recent example: this one is more hypothetical, since the movie is still quite new, but I believe that the last story in "Resurrection" by Bi Gan (the one that is one uninterrupted 40-minute long take) will probably be remembered as a towering achievement of filmmaking for those in the know, also overshadowing the rest of the film (even though I overall quite like the rest). It's simply filmmaking virtuousism, the type of scene that you get to witness perhaps only once a decade. I would be surprised if is not studied to hell and back in film school classes in the future.
6. potentially lynch's entry in 'lumière & company'— i feel like i've seen it overshadow most of the other segments in online discussions. that's especially seeing as it was released separately in a collection of his short films & there being such a cult of personality around him

## 3. Obsession: Male Passivity

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Throughout recent horror, there have been plenty examples of the genre being used as a vehicle to critique toxic masculine traits. However, a majority of these traits have characteristics associated with the extroverted type — the male who abuses with assertiveness, usually related to violent or sexual acts.

However, what I love about Obsession is that it explores toxic traits from the other angle. Mainly, rather than the extroverted assertiveness, this is all about the problematic nature of introverted passivity. Throughout the whole entire narrative, Bear’s common trope is the failure to act upon any situations that require the proper moral response. After the wish occurs and Nikki loses all free-will, Bear really assumes an authoritarian position in moulding someone under his own desires rather than their own accord. And what is so morally dubious about the entire dynamic is that despite seeing the suppressed Nikki who is clearly suffering from a loss of anatomy (the phone call scene I think is the scariest for this reason), he fails to act at every turn. Even his own suicide attempt is botched. In some sense, I think the film is very much about calling to the development of male courage and taking moral responsibility for your actions, rather than just passively letting the world abide as it is and allowing people to suffer.

And when Nikki finally wakes up, this is surely a metaphor for someone in an abusive relationship who wakes up to their own circumstances. There’s countless evidence of people performing a sense of cognitive dissonance to justify their relationship only to look back retrospectively and understand how they were manipulated the whole time.

Anyway, brilliant film and highly original in its critique of male passivity. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I partially agree, but not completely. 

I find that a lot of the online discourse around the movie hyper-fixates on what Bear should do, without acknowledging that Bear is also in an abusive relationship with a controlling, manipulative monster. It’s a monster of his own making, for sure, but every step his actions have to be considered through the lens of somebody who is trapped. 

Note that early on he doesn’t realize that “real Nikki” is trapped in there. There’s enough for the audience to pick up on that, but initially Bear doesn’t even believe it was the wish causing the change. When Nikki has a moment of seeming out of sorts and uncomfortable (when we see real-Nikki slip through), his immediate response is to back off and confirm consent. When he learns that she’s lied to him to drive his sympathy, his response is to walk away, but he’s accused of ruining a good moment, and through that is coerced into further and further intimacy. 

At every stage, false Nikki strips away Bear’s agency and further positions him under her thumb. She uses his concern for her to suggest self-harm if he tries to leave, and once he knows the cause she uses his making the wish as further leverage over him, making her violence his “fault.” In the same way that we acknowledge that an abused woman staying with her abuser is the wrong move, but we understand how fraught it is, how dangerous a situation to navigate, and how completely broken her will may be to reach that point; we also have to acknowledge that Bear’s inability to walk away is more complicated than just boiling it down to cherry picked lines of dialogue about how he doesn’t want to give it up. Again—“I just want them to really love me” is a hallmark of the abused. 

And it’s wild to me how many people point out his not killing himself—or her, when she requests it—as this monumental failing. I highly doubt anyone saying he should do either of those things would find themselves in a similar situation and saying “oh of course, this is logically the most responsible course to take.”

None of this is meant to let Bear off the hook for his genuine responsibility, either. In fact, where I agree with you is in what this tells us about the monkey’s paw aspect of the story. Bear wishes for love, and what he gets is the kind of intense jealousy typical of toxic masculinity and the incel crowd that treats affection as something they’re entitled to. In other words, the One Wish Willow gives him an extreme version of the kind of infatuation that Bear himself mistakes for love. Hence why fake-Nikki really is a monster of his own making. It’s not just the Willow giving him a twisted version of what he wanted, it’s that what Bear wanted to be to Nikki was always a problem, and instead he’s having that reflected back on himself, and comes face to face with the sense of cruel entitlement that he’d never let himself understand was in it. 
  - Amazing analysis. Do you have a blog or a Substack?
  - Idk man, I think the problem with this reading is that it turns Bear into a co-victim in a way that obscures the actual moral centre of the film. This reframes the entire power dynamic around Bear’s emotional experience in a way that ends up minimising what’s actually happening to Nikki. By being so focused on arguing that Bear is “also abused” you've flattened the moral distinction between the person being stripped of autonomy and the person knowingly benefiting from that loss of autonomy.

What makes *Obsession* disturbing is precisely that Bear’s suffering does not erase his responsibility. The horror is that he keeps choosing comfort over confronting the reality in front of him. The horror works precisely because Bear is sympathetic. If he were just cartoonishly malicious, the film wouldn’t have nearly as much to say. Even once he understands Nikki is trapped, he continues to participate because some part of him still prefers the fantasy of being loved to the pain of letting her go. That’s the core moral failure.

And honestly, I think calling fake Nikki his “abuser” muddies the metaphor in a pretty ugly way. Fake Nikki isn’t an independent person victimising him; she’s literally the manifestation of his wish. She's not an independent manipulative force acting upon him. She embodies his possessiveness, entitlement, insecurity, and desire for unconditional devotion. Treating her like an external abusive girlfriend feels like a way of displacing responsibility away from him and onto a feminized monster figure that he himself created.

The “he’s trapped too” argument also ignores the massive asymmetry of harm. Bear still retains agency throughout the film. Nikki does not. He can hesitate, rationalise, panic, freeze, self-pity, or fail morally. He still has the capacity to act! Nikki is the one whose consciousness and autonomy are being erased. Equating those experiences is kinda wild.

You're recentering the conversation onto the feelings of the man who benefits from the abuse rather than the woman experiencing it. Especially because the film already goes out of its way to show how men can weaponise passivity. The point is that harm can come from cowardice, self-delusion, and refusal to relinquish control just as much as overt aggression. At the end of the day, Bear still has free-will, and he has stripped Nikki of both her own selfhood and her free-will.

Lowkey your reply accidentally demonstrates the exact thing the original post is criticising: the instinct to endlessly empathise with male paralysis while the woman’s loss of self becomes secondary.

## 4. My qualms about rating films

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Now this is strictly my experience. I'm not imparting any judgement or advice on anyone. But over the past year I've found that rating films have proven to be an exercise in futility. And judging by many other people's systems, I have found that my problems are these:

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

• **No rating system is equally balanced**. There's virtually no difference between 10 and a 9, only an arbitrary decision of which films you love a little bit more. On the contrary, there's a **huge** difference between 7 and 8, as 7 just feels like a nice way to say "yeah it's okay, pretty good" whereas 8 is an all around great rating. But we go back again as there's not much difference between a 6 and a 7, both mean "*I liked it*" with the former leaning a bit to the negative and the latter a bit to the positive.

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

• The overall mechanism of ratings creates a vague hierarchy, an arbitrary one at that. It imparts that some films are superior or inferior to others, which just simply is not the case unless it's genuinely morally corrupted. I understand this is opinion based, but even so, in my mind it was getting cemented in an objective way which I didn't like.

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

• Ratings create the allure of an acquired or intellectual taste, so much so that I've found some people (myself included) will scrutinize films to the point of nitpicking minor details to justify their 5/10 or 6/10. Whereas had that energy (at least mine) been spent appreciating the better aspects of said film rather than something like "minor pacing issues in the third act", I would've felt more fulfilled. 

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

• It can never properly express how I truly feel, this is what bugs me the most. Now, for example, I love the first *Kingsman* movie to death, I can rewatch it over and over, all day— I would not get bored. But I couldn't for the life of me bring myself to give it a score above 8, why? Because it's frankly just not that great. That's not the point. My point is, how am I supposed to balance my own opinion and a more "objective" viewpoint with a rating? I found that I simply can't. 

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

Happy to discuss with people who have a rating system that works for them perfectly.

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m someone who takes my Letterboxd ratings rather seriously, I put a good amount of thought into them and rarely give out the highest ratings as I want those to feel special. All that is to say, none of it really matters as no one really cares about my rating system other than myself. It’s really for my satisfaction and is primarily a reference point to recall what I thought about a film once I likely forget most of the specifics. 

There’s really not an “objective” way to do it since it’s all subjective at the end of the day. I personally find it fun to quibble about and consider my and my friends’s ratings, but it’s not that serious. I even go as far as to create absurd ranked lists as in exercise in how arbitrary this all is. Don’t overthink it, unless you have fun doing that then by all means overthink it. 
  - To add on to your point I have poor memory so its a good reference point for me to know how I felt about the movie at that time and comparing it to now if I would rewatch the movie. Its our subjective list after all and not a definitive ranking.
2. >"There's virtually no difference between 10 and a 9, only an arbitrary decision of which films you love a little bit more. On the contrary, there's a **huge** difference between 7 and 8, as 7 just feels like a nice way to say "yeah it's okay, pretty good" whereas 8 is an all around great rating."

They're both a tenth difference, heh. I'd say they're equally impactful.

That said, I also dislike number ratings for films a lot. I've been using Letterboxd to track my films and hate giving them ratings. 
3. I’ve stopped adding numerical or even thumbs up and thumbs down ratings. I initially gave Heat an 8/10 but now it’s one of my favorites. I think that initial number came from me feeling like I needed to give it one.

I try to take a few notes but most of my “reviews” are in relation to the person/situation of me talking. That being said, I do note if I really liked a movie.
4. In just about every context a rating is ultimately condensing multiple axes of assessment down to a single number/ranking. An 8/10 conveys that you think it's pretty good, but one 8/10 may be a movie you loved and had tons of fun watching but isn’t technically great, another may be a technical masterpiece that you just don’t love regardless of how much you acknowledge its achievement.

Personally, I’m comfortable with that reality - for one, I think knowing things like the genre and director of a movie already gives you a decent idea of what "8/10" probably means for that particular film, but figuring out *exactly* what it means is what the text of the review is for.

If you can't find it in yourself to be comfortable with that, you're free to write reviews without a rating attached
5. I think it's simple, if someone is using five star system, and they give it two, that means that they on some level liked less than half of the film's qualities. The closer to 5 they get the more they liked about it, and if they don't rate it perfectly then they saw a few faults in it that prevented them from given it a perfect score. Their rating along with a written review would clear things up a bit more. But the rating gives me a sense of whether they liked a movie or not and if they hated or loved it. What they value in a film in general is usually detailed in the written review. 
6. I used to throw a tizzy when someone threw out a number rating, but it's pretty inconsequential. The worst thing about them is that they're pretty superfluous. If I'm on Letterboxd, I'll care more about what a review says than how many stars a user gave it. If all someone has to say about a movie is that it's a 9/10, then the problem here is that they're not really engaging with the material enough to say anything of value. It's the same as just saying "It's good" and leaving it at that.

## 5. Obsession: Did bear do anything wrong? Or are we asking the wrong question?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** The movie starts off asking a moral question in the shape of Bear's wish. Would he still have wished, knowing there was 100% guarantee. Barker doesn't give us the opportunity to decide. He doesn't wish that Nikki pays him any attention, that she take him out, even that she fall in love with him. 

He just wishes that she would love him more than she had loved anyone before. 

This makes me begin to wonder if the film wants us to condemn the outcome of the wish or the wish itself. Is Bear the type of guy who owns things, and is the fantasy merely bringing out the truth? Or is he a lonely outsider whom when his fantasy becomes real, turns in to the beast from the abyss of his desires. The movie itself seems unwilling to resolve this debate.

Bear is too pathetic to be labelled the villian and yet the wish is too revealing for it to be a simple case of a lonely, romantic wishing he had someone to share his life with. If Bear had known the consequences of the wish, would he have still made it?

If no, what does this imply about him? If yes, then what does that imply about the film?

There's subtextual and visual allusions to rape, and what he is doing to her is rape as far as her body (and original Nikki) is concerned. He is a bad guy.

      - Can sympathy and condemnation coexist? I think the film depends on them coexisting. If Bear were completely unsympathetic, why spend so much time establishing his loneliness before the wish?
    - Perhaps because Barker finds it more interesting to explore the evil in the banality of Bear's weakness.

I feel like the opening tries hard to get the audience to sympathize with him so that when he starts to make selfish and evil choices it hooks everyone who empathized with him in the first act. 

It's a clever manipulation of the audience. 

      - That's a compelling reading. If Bear is designed as an ordinary, sympathetic person rather than an obvious villain, does the wish reveal something uniquely wrong with him, or something Barker thinks is common in human desire?
